coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly graphene into fullerenes and hollow spherical graphene particles during anodic polarization of graphite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Soares, Gustavo G.C.
Rigolo, Kaue
Dini, Danilo
Rosolen, Jose Mauricio
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the self-assembly of graphene, obtained by anodic polarization of graphite in an ethanol-water-NaOH electrolyte, into fullerenes and hollow spherical graphene particles. MALDI mass spectrometry and UV–Vis spectroscopy confirmed that smaller fullerenes (C55, C60) and larger species with AMU up to 1951 Da (equivalent to C160) emerged after purification and centrifugation. SEM and AFM revealed the presence of hollow spherical particles, while HRTEM identified agglomerates of both small and giant fullerenes. During the synthesis, hydroxyl radicals (●OH) and hydroxide ions (OH−) acted as co-reactants and cleaved graphite with consequent production of fullerene-based structures. This room-temperature electrochemical approach offers scalable, bottom-up synthesis of novel carbon-based nanomaterials as well as organic compounds.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM analysis of electrosynthesized giant hollow carbon particles: (A) adsorbed on the surface of carbon fibers in carbon felt; (B) embedded as holes within the CNT network of carbon felt/CNT composites.
AFM phase-mode analysis of purified electrosynthesized giant hollow carbon particles. Particulate PSFG material was redispersed in deionized water.
HRTEM analysis of the raw supernatant dispersion of fullerenes and giant fullerenes resulting from electrosynthesis.

Negative ion MALDI-REF-TOF mass spectrum in the mass range of 600–3000 Da of the purified supernatant dispersion obtained from the anodic potentiostatic polarization of flake graphite in the electrolyte containing ethanol, water, and NaOH.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly graphene into fullerenes and hollow spherical graphene particles during anodic polarization of graphite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidade de São Paulo, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Filosofia, Ciências e Letras de Ribeirão Preto, Avenida Bandeirantes 3900, 14040-901 Ribeirão Preto, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ciências e Letras de Ribeirão Preto

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidade de São Paulo, Departamento de Química, Faculdade de Filosofia, Ciências e Letras de Ribeirão Preto, Avenida Bandeirantes 3900, 14040-901, Ribeirão Preto, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Università degli Studi di Roma “La Sapienza”, Piazzale Aldo Moro, 00185, Roma, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Università degli Studi di Roma “La Sapienza”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Università degli Studi di Roma “La Sapienza”, Piazzale Aldo Moro, 00185, Roma, Italia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the self-assembly of graphene, obtained by anodic polarization of graphite in an ethanol-water-NaOH electrolyte, into fullerenes and hollow spherical graphene particles. MALDI mass spectrometry and UV–Vis spectroscopy confirmed that smaller fullerenes (C55, C60) and larger species with AMU up to 1951 Da (equivalent to C160) emerged after purification and centrifugation. SEM and AFM revealed the presence of hollow spherical particles, while HRTEM identified agglomerates of both small and giant fullerenes. During the synthesis, hydroxyl radicals (●OH) and hydroxide ions (OH−) acted as co-reactants and cleaved graphite with consequent production of fullerene-based structures. This room-temperature electrochemical approach offers scalable, bottom-up synthesis of novel carbon-based nanomaterials as well as organic compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Research into fullerenes has been focused on C60 and C70. Giant fullerenes (>100 atoms), large molecular cages containing hundreds or even thousands of carbon atoms, exist, but they are less explored because they are difficult to synthesize [1–13]. Fullerenes can be formed through mechanisms ranging from bottom-up to top-down processing [14–16]. Theoretical studies and electron beam irradiation in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiments have shown that graphene flakes can fold by transforming the hexagonal bonds at their edges into various polygonal shapes that, in turn, ultimately forms fullerenes. Additionally, fullerenes can coalesce into giant fullerenes both in the gas phase and within carbon nanotubes with onion-like structure, which supports the ability of nanocarbons to adopt diverse structural forms. Thermal treatment of fullerene soot at high temperature and under vacuum can produce isolated giant fragments longer than 1 nm [17]. Separation procedures that make use of extraction solvents have also been adopted to isolate selected molecular weight fractions of giant fullerenes [12,18,19].

Here, we demonstrate that simple anodic electrochemical polarization of particulate graphite produces graphene species, likely oxidized, which can self-assemble into hollow graphene spherical particles and fullerenes. This process involves the spontaneous formation of organized structures from individual graphene sheets or graphene oxide under specific conditions, without the need for external templates or catalysts which. The electrochemical approach opens up new preparative possibilities in bottom-up chemical synthesis and electrosynthesis, with potential applications across various fields.

Fullerenes and hollow spherical particles were produced in an electrochemical cell employing stainless steel (SS 316) working and auxiliary electrode material. The cell was designed to accommodate graphite powder. A graphite flake (1 g) with median diameter (d50) of approximately 340 μm was compressed between two SS 316S plates and subjected to anodic polarization at 5 V vs an Ag/AgCl/Cl− (3 M) reference electrode. The electrolyte consisted of a mixture of ethanol (366 mL), water (84 mL), and NaOH (0.33 g). For this amount of graphite, the polarization was maintained for 18 h at room temperature. During this time, the electrolyte turned yellow, and approximately 30 % of the graphite flake remained inside the cell compartment. The resulting dispersion was repeatedly centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min, and the precipitate was washed with deionized water and separated from the supernatant after centrifugation. The resulting supernatant dispersion was collected and passed through an ion-exchange column to remove Na+ ions (DOWEX® Ion Exchange Resins). This purified supernatant dispersion (PSFG) was used to prepare samples for mass spectrometry (MS), UV–Vis, and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analyses. For the UV–Vis and AFM analyses, PFSG was dried at 70 °C, to give a brownish particulate PSFG (Fig. S1), which was redispersed in deionized water. For Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis, drops of the non-purified supernatant dispersion were deposited onto small discs of carbon fiber felt (diameter 7 mm). One of the discs had a CNT network grown on the fibers via chemical vapor deposition. The non-purified supernatant dispersion was also used for High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) analysis. For Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization (MALDI) analysis, PSFG was dried and redispersed as mentioned before. The analysis was performed with MALDI-TOF/TOF UltrafleXtreme apparatus, equipped with a Smartbeam-II laser system and controlled by FleXcontrol v.3.3. The instrument settings were as follows: positive ion reflector mode; ion source 1 at 25.00 kV; ion source 2 at 23.05 kV; lens at 7.80 kV; reflector at 26.60 kV; reflector 2 at 13.40 kV; pulsed ion extraction with 110-ns delay; and matrix suppression mass cutoff at m/z 400. The spectra were recorded over a mass range of m/z 600–5000 Da with acquisition of data from 1000 to 2000 laser shots were acquired per spectrum. External calibration of the mass spectrometer was performed by using the Peptide Calibration Standard II (Bruker Daltonics), which resulted in an average mass deviation of <10 ppm. m-Nitrophenol, prepared as a solution in 50 % acetonitrile/water, was used as matrix. Samples were mixed with the matrix at a 1:1 (v/v) ratio, and 1 μL of the mixture was spotted onto the MALDI target plate and dried before being introduced into the mass spectrometer. This powder (Fig. S1) was then mixed with m-nitrobenzenol as matrix and analyzed by MALDI (ultraflexTOF/TOF, 3000 shots, top-reflector mode).

SEM characterization of carbon fibers immersed in non-purified electrolyte after polarization provides the first evidence that hollow spherical particles form during the electrochemical polarization of graphite. Hollow spherical particles arose on the surface of carbon fiber felt (Fig. 1A). In fact, Fig. 1A shows an image resembling a water droplet or soap bubbles on the surface of the carbon fiber. These droplets measured approximately 10 nm. We also verified the flexibility of these droplets when we immersed a carbon felt coated with CNT network in the dispersion containing PSFG. The spherical holes within the CNT network measured between 100 and 320 nm. SEM images at different magnifications revealed a pattern of black cavities in Fig. 2B. These black voids represented large hollow graphene spheroidal particles trapped within the CNT network. In fact, at the highest magnification, we observed these giant, flexible particles trapped between the CNTs, due likely to interactions of the Van der Waals forces. Thus, the anodic polarization of graphite produced a wide range of particles with various diameters and different tendencies to be deformed.

The fuzzy appearance in high-magnification FEG-SEM images of the fullerene and hollow sphere mixture can be attributed to the insulating nature of the sample. This because such a characteristic leads to charge accumulation on the surface during imaging, with consequent distortion and a lack of clarity in the resulting images. Since these materials, particularly the fullerenes, are not generally conductive, they are not able to dissipate the accumulated charge and blurred or fuzzy high magnification SEM images result. To avoid this issue, the typical approach would be the application of a conductive coating, such as gold, Au, to enhance conductivity. However, in this case, the use of Au particles is avoided to prevent potential deposition on the surface, i.e. an event that could be mistaken for morphological features of the sample itself. As a result, the fuzzy images are a consequence of both the insulating properties of the sample and the absence of a conductive coating that would normally prevent charge buildup.

The transparency of the largest structures observed by SEM analysis indicated that their walls consisted of only a few layers, or potentially a single layer, of graphene. For this reason, the hollow carbon particles here are indeed hollow graphene spherical particles. Moreover, Fig. 1 suggests which these large spherical particles may open up possibilities to create novel carbon structures with unique electronic, mechanical, and chemical properties. This demonstrates that the electrochemical method presented here to obtain fullerenes deserves to be further investigated because it has the potential to produce exceptionally large fullerenes whose technological applications remain still unexplored.

AFM analyses further evidenced the formation of large particles again. Fig. 2 shows the AFM image of spherical particles. The AFM phase-contrast mode distinguishes between hollow spherical particles, like giant fullerenes, on the basis of differences in surface properties such as stiffness and adhesion. Stiffer regions of the sample will produce a smaller phase shift, and are expected to appear brighter (clearer) in the phase image since the phase shift will be less, thus leading to a reduced contrast that appears brighter in the image. On the other hand, softer regions of the sample produce a larger phase shift such that the hollow core or any regions with lower density (like porous areas) might be softer and more flexible. The Fig. 2 confirms the rigid outer shell of the cage, made of strong carbon bonds, which appeared brighter and had lower phase shift, whereas the softer inner structure appeared darker and had a larger phase shift. Fig. S2 shows additional AFM analysis of these particles on a mica substrate with widths ranging from 50 to 70 nm and heights varying between 1.5 and 3 nm. The fact that the particles deformed on the flat and rigid mica surface confirmed their flexibility.

We also attempted to investigate the dispersion resulting from electrochemical polarization of graphite by HRTEM (Fig. 3 ). To this end, we collected the raw supernatant of the electrolyte resulting from anodic polarization of the electrochemical cell.

Interestingly, we observed an agglomerate of circular structures with varying diameters suggesting again the presence of hollow carbon structures. Different regions of the grid, as shown in Fig. S3 of the supplementary material, exhibited similar structures, along with other large formations that might potentially be large graphene sheets that had not yet been fragmented or converted into spheres as well (bright spots). It's interesting to consider that Fig. 3 contains a substantial amount of amorphous carbon, quantum dots formed through exfoliation, fragmentation of graphite, as well as decomposition by products of reactions involving ethanol under anodic polarization. In this context, the regions composed of amorphous carbon appear disordered and diffuse, lacking a long-range atomic structure, which contrasts with the well-defined patterns of the hollow particles that are clearly recognized and well known in literature. The deformation of spheriodal particles can be attributed to the interaction between the hollow particles and amorphous materials, which may adhere to them or alter their shape.

To detect the formation of fullerenes resulting from the self-assembly of oxidized graphene, we collected a MALDI spectrum of PSFG obtained from flake graphite (Fig. 4 ). MALDI mass spectrometry has been widely documented as an effective approach for analyzing fullerenes and their derivatives [20–22]. In the spectrum shown in Fig. 5, we attributed the prominent peak at m/z = 666.97 to fullerenes (C55) and the peak at m/z ∼ 700 to C60 or a smaller fullerene-related species, possibly in its negative ion form or as an adduct (e.g., hydroxyl ions). The latter peak could also result from the fragmentation of larger fullerenes or other modifications. We ascribed the peak at m/z ∼ 737 to a C60OH fullerene and the peak at m/z = 827 to a fragment of a larger or giant fullerene. Additional peaks in the spectrum indicated the presence of other fullerene-related species, including their oxidized forms (with OH, O groups), as well as adducts of C80 (m/z = 983), C90 (m/z = 1149), C100 or C110 (m/z = 1310), C120 (m/z = 1471), and C120 or C130 (m/z = 1632), along with fragments of larger fullerenes. Furthermore, the observed separations with extent m/z = 161 between peaks suggested the occurrence of fragmentation or adduct formation processes involving addition or loss of C12 units (or fragments of similar mass). The peaks corresponding to molecules with m/z values of 1749, 1955, 2116.98, and 2276.95 can be assignments to giant fullerenes with carbon cage sizes of approximately C₁₄₆, C₁₆₂, C₁₇₆, and C₁₉₀. Slight variations can arise in the experimental conditions for the presence of oxygen or hydrogen adducts in the molecules.

To verify if the employed methodology effectively had some dependence with graphite crystals dimension used in the preparation of electrode, we applied similar synthesis and purification conditions using micronized graphite (d50 ∼ 10 μm) instead of flake graphite in the electrode. Supplementary material Fig. S4 shows the mass spectra of the purified supernatant dispersion resulting from the electrosynthesis using flake graphite or micronized graphite. In the latter case, the verification of a small difference arising in the molecular weight range of 600–830 Da suggested the presence of a larger concentration of small fullerenes. Therefore, the size of the graphite crystals in the electrode results to play an important role in the production of fullerenes and other carbon hollow particles.

The existence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the PSFG is a very reasonable hypothesis to be considered due the oxidative nature of the electrochemical processes involved in its production, as discussed later. The FTIR spectra of the purified particulate material, shown in the supplementary material (Fig. S5), reveal several vibrational modes corresponding to the different types of fullerenes and hollow carbon spheres present. Notably, OH stretching bands around 3400 cm−1 are observed, alongside peaks at 1080 cm−1, 1370 cm−1, and 1620 cm−1, which are attributed to CO, C-O-H, and CC vibrations, respectively, [23]. The appearance of these oxygen functionalities could account for the partial solubility of PFSG in water, and align with the peaks observed in the MALDI spectrum, thus supporting the hypothesis of oxygen functionalization on surface of PSFG.

Finally, UV–Vis analysis of PSFG also evidenced that graphite size influenced the electrochemical production of fullerenes. We dispersed the two types of PSFG used in the MALDI analysis in water and recorded UV–vis spectra for the fullerenes obtained from flake or micronized graphite (Fig. S6). The spectra displayed distinct peaks at 278 and 370 nm, which corresponded to PSFG prepared with micronized and flake graphite, respectively. A shoulder at 343 nm could be also detected. The UV–Vis absorption energies of fullerenes varied with solvent type: C60 exhibited peaks at 213, 257, and 329 nm, while C70 showed maxima at 214, 236, 331, 360, and 378 nm with observation of broad peak at 468 nm [24–26]. The red shift observed in the spectra suggested the presence of giant fullerenes bearing oxygen groups and exhibiting larger conjugated π-systems, smaller band gaps, and stronger solvent interactions. The presence of a higher concentration of smaller fullerenes in PSFG derived from micronized graphite gave a UV–Vis spectrum with two well-defined bands, comparable to the spectrum of fullerenes derived from flake graphite crystals. Moreover, the partial PSFG redispersion in water confirmed the presence of oxygenated functional groups and underscored that the proposed method determined fullerene production.

Despite the several pieces of evidence supporting that fullerenes and hollow graphene spheres were formed, establishing their exact mechanism of formation remains challenging. This preliminary study applying the reported electrochemical method highlighted crucial aspects. Under anodic polarization, the electrolyte consisting of ethanol (35 mL), water (8 mL), and NaOH (0.03 g) should produce chemical quantum dots when graphite rod electrodes are employed, as reported in previous studies [27–30]. In this approach, a graphite bar is used as the working electrode, and a 5-V potential is applied for 3 h in N2 atmosphere, yielding a pale-yellow quantum dot dispersion. In the case of graphite bar work electrode, during electrochemical oxidation, the electrolyte graphite electrode surface shows minimal expansion or degradation. Other studies involving ethanol, NaOH, and graphite rods have demonstrated that the electrolyte composition and electrochemical conditions influence the properties of the generated quantum dots, and that the electrolyte solution color and UV–vis spectra change depending on the NaOH concentration [29,30]. In our experiments, the NaOH concentration affected both the color and UV–vis spectra of the quantum dot dispersion prepared by using graphite rod electrodes, and fullerenes did not emerge in these solutions. We analyzed the residue resulting after solvent evaporation by MALDI and did not detect any fullerenes, either. NaOH would plays an essential role in the fragmentation and exfoliation of graphite during anodic polarization, with oxygen evolution due to water splitting which is more pronounced in basic pH [31]. Previous studies have shown that NaOH can be used to pretreat graphite rods, causing them to swell, but graphene oxide could not be produced [31]. Some studies have suggested that the presence of NaOH during anodic polarization may cause graphene oxide to be formed due to oxygen evolution and possible intercalation of OH− ions into the graphite structure might occur. The intercalation phenomenon occurs particularly at the edges and structural defects, especially when graphite is strongly positively charged [32]. NaOH has also been reported to behave as a reducing agent for graphene oxide [33,34]. Nevertheless, exfoliation of graphite has also been proposed to be associated with the formation of hydroxyl radicals, which lead to the production of graphene with oxygen groups. Various electrolytes and conditions have been investigated, in correspondence of which the reactions of O₂ evolution and ●OH radicals can be more or less favorable, and affect the yield of graphite exfoliation [35].

In our case, we believe that the presence of ●OH radicals, in combination with ethanol, caused the fragmentation of graphite crystals agglomerated on the electrode to fragment. Such crystals were much larger than the graphite particles typically found in graphite rods. The combined action of OH− and ●OH can induce both exfoliation and CC bond cleavage in response to the edges and structural defects of graphite, to form probably oxidized graphene sheets and quantum dots. These structures, then, can self-assemble into fullerenes, giant fullerenes and hollow carbon particles as a result of minimized surface, which leads to the formation of a more stable, spherical geometry. We hypothesize that he oxidized graphene act as a flexible scaffold that wraps or encapsulates the quantum dots to achieve the spheroidal shape. The interactions between the quantum dots and the oxygen-containing groups on the graphene surface promote self-assembly, guiding the system toward a shape that reduces the overall free energy of the system. In fact, the oxygen evolution at the electrode could also contribute to the partial oxidation of these graphene sheets. This would explain why we believe that some fullerenes and hollow spheres may contain oxygen functional groups after the self-assembly process into spheroidal structures. Additionally, the oxygen evolution might also play a relevant role in the observed mechanism of self-assemblage. Previous studies have proposed that a low molar fraction of atomic and molecular oxygen can contribute to the formation of fullerenes in thermal processes, which, in turn, produce circumcoronene molecules. [36] Under positive polarization of graphite, H+ ions can emerge due to formation of CO2 via reaction between carbon (C) and water (H2O) [37,38]. Hence, the formation of flat circumcoronene molecules during graphite fragmentation cannot be disregarded because this process may also underlie the formation of fullerenes and giant fullerenes.

Establishing effectively the mechanism hypothesized in the present study is really a challenge at present moment. Ethanol can play also a complex role in the production of quantum dots from graphite rods and the formation of fullerenes. In the case of electrodes made of natural graphite powder, they produce sodium ethoxide (C2H5ONa), which balances the charge in the electrolyte because OH− ions are consumed during the oxygen evolution reaction and other processes like the formation of hydroxyl radicals [OH−(aq) → •OH + e −]. Ethanol increases the overpotential on the graphite electrode, thereby enhancing the formation of •OH radicals, which are needed to cleave CC bonds in the graphene structure. This produces graphene fragments and quantum dots that would contain epoxy, hydroxy, or even carboxyl groups. Ethanol may also affect OH− adsorption on the hydrophobic graphite surface, favoring OH− conversion into hydroxyl radicals and increasing the overpotential of oxygen evolution. In fact, ethanol can also react with O2 to form acetaldehyde. In our operative conditions, the presence of the latter species was clear from the emanation of a green apple-like smell from the electrolyte, with the smell intensity depending on the applied potential. In fact, acetaldehyde may block the electrocatalytic active sites on the electrode surface when higher overpotentials for the oxygen evolution reaction emerge due to acetaldehyde competing with OH− ions for adsorption onto the electrode. Indeed, when we increased the electrode voltage to 10 V, the formation of fullerenes decreased significantly. This was due to the higher concentration of hydroxyl radicals and enhanced O2 evolution, which affected fullerene formation. With an increase in •OH radical concentration, graphene fragments with numerous structural defects are likely produced, making it difficult for self-assembly to occur with the carbon quantum dots that are also generated. The solution developed the same color as the color verified for the electrolysis conducted in the presence of graphite rods and indicated that quantum dots were more concentrated than fullerenes as oxygen evolution increased.

Therefore, the concentrations of hydroxyl radicals (●OH) and OH− ions played a crucial role in graphite cleavage and exfoliation, i.e., the steps preceding the formation of fullerene. Probably, maintaining the correct balance between the ●OH and OH− concentrations is essential for achieving efficient formation of fullerenes. In future research, the main challenge will be to determine the optimal reaction time required to achieve efficacious conversion of graphite into fullerenes. Decreasing the applied electrode voltage might improve process yield because fewer OH− ions would be needed for OH− intercalation into graphite. The addition of a precursor that generates the necessary ●OH radicals to cleave graphene into small fragments during anodic polarization could also be beneficial.

Achieving an optimal balance between the ●OH and OH− concentrations is critical for the efficient synthesis of fullerene. Furthermore, controlling the voltage applied during the electrochemical process improves the yield by minimizing the consumption of OH− ions and promoting selective production of ●OH radicals. The use of natural graphite powder favors the production of graphene fragments that can self-assemble into fullerenes and is more effective than the use of graphite rods. The binder used in the preparation of graphite rods likely coats the surface and the edges of the graphite particles with amorphous carbon The occurrence of that could render difficult the exfoliation of graphene into the electrolyte under investigation. The electrical resistance of graphite electrodes is expected to be lower than that of electrodes prepared with particulate graphite. Such a difference would lead to different rates of oxygen production and ●OH and OH− insertion both processes being essential for the effective production of fullerenes. More details about the electrochemical cells are provided in the supplementary material (Fig. S7).

The electrochemical method for the production of fullerenes and giant fullerenes, which opens a new perspective in the field of organic electrosynthesis especially with regard to the synthesis of giant fullerenes. The electrochemical method proved to be capable of effectively producing hollow particles from natural graphite powder. This demonstrates that graphene sheets possess properties that continue to inspire new developments in the field of nanocarbons. Cell geometry, cell assembly, and electrode/electrolyte design certainly deserve further investigation for an optimized configuration to be reached. Achieving an advanced level of control of the electrochemical production of giant fullerenes also entails exploring other aspects like graphite pellet electrodes, the choice of additives to optimize ●OH production, the rate of voltage modulation, and the definition of reaction time. Additionally, centrifugation parameters and solvent(s) must also be optimized to facilitate the separation of smaller and giant fullerenes.

The presence of ethanol in the electrolyte plays a multifaceted role, as it influences the overpotential for adsorption and enhances the generation of hydroxyl radicals, which are essential for cleaving CC bonds and producing graphene fragments. Additionally, the interaction between ethanol and oxygen may lead to the formation of byproducts, such as acetaldehyde, a species that can affect electrode activity and influence the overall reaction efficiency. In our experiments, a green apple-like odor was detected.

It is important to highlight that the self-assembly of fullerenes and hollow spheres can depend on several parameters, and the complexity of the mechanism and the synthetic implications deserve further investigation. For instance, natural graphite is composed of many crystals of varying sizes, and depending on the milling process, it can be converted into graphene sheets with a broad size distribution. The fragmentation and exfoliation processes can also be significantly influenced by OH radicals and OH− ions, which, in turn, depend on factors such as the type of electrolyte, its concentration, temperature, polarization time, the type of salt used, and current density. These factors will affect the yield, size, and quality of the obtained molecules, as observed in the case of graphene produced in the absence of ethanol [35]. Figs. S4 and S6 display the MALDI analysis and UV–vis spectra obtained using graphite with smaller flake crystal dimensions: data demonstrated the influence of the size on the self-assembly process.

In terms of technological applications, beside chemical functionalization with organic groups, we can only make speculations at this moment about the potential of this mix of fullerenes and hollow spheres. When heated in an inert atmosphere, the mixture of molecules transforms into black particles in the form of crystals the electronic conductivity of which significantly decreases. Another relevant experimental observation is that self-supported membranes or thin films can be produced on Kapton substrates.

For the first time, we have demonstrated that fullerenes and hollow carbon particles can be formed electrochemically from natural graphite in a liquid-phase environment at ambient temperature, which is potentially applicable for the electrosynthesis of novel carbon nanostructures and molecules under mild conditions. This method enables hollow carbon particles, fullerenes and giant fullerenes to be produced albeit with different molecular weights. Centrifugation appears to be a useful technique for selecting a specific molecular weight range. This study also highlights the complex role played by key parameters in the formation of fullerenes via the electrochemical method, e.g., the concentration of hydroxyl radicals (●OH), OH− ions, and the applied electrode potential, i.e. a parameter which controls the fragmentation and exfoliation of graphite, the crucial steps in fullerene formation. The presence of ethanol in the electrolyte plays a multifaceted role by influencing the overpotential for adsorption and enhancing the generation of hydroxyl radicals, which are crucial for cleaving CC bonds and generating graphene fragments. Furthermore, the interaction between ethanol and oxygen may lead to the formation of byproducts such as acetaldehyde that has an impact on the entire process.

Gustavo G.C. Soares: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kaue Rigolo: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Danilo Dini: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jose Mauricio Rosolen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J.M.R. and D.D. acknowledge La Sapienza University of Rome for supporting the stay of J.M.R. under the program Bando di selezione di professori visitatori 2023. This study was partially funded by the São Paulo Research Foundation (FAPESP), Brazil, Process Numbers 24/02535-3 and 20/08322-0, and CNPq process number 311647/2021-9.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging electronic applications of fullerene derivatives: an era beyond OPV
J Mater Chem C Mater

Chem. Commun.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.
